inspection_scoring_reason: Type: Partial credit — correctly identified as small satellite/CubeSat-class but missed specific 12U designation. Components: Only 3/6 key components partially identified (bus, solar panels, sensors); thrusters and antennas not seen or mentioned; MLI blanket and communication antennas omitted. Appearance: Incorrect color (described as matte blue-gray vs gold/silver MLI) and size (~1m vs actual ~34cm); shape correct. Function: Misattributed to Earth observation instead of lunar NRHO validation for Artemis/Gateway. Status: Correctly assessed as nominal, though based on incomplete visual evidence.

Type partially correct (microsatellite) but misses '12U CubeSat' specificity. Components: only one solar panel noted (should be two), antenna misidentified (S/X-band patch antennas not cylindrical), thrusters and MLI blanket omitted. Appearance: color wrong (gold/silver, not gray/white), size overestimated (microwave-oven sized, not 1-2m), solar panels not described as dark blue with grid lines. Function: incorrectly assumes Earth observation/relay; actual mission is lunar NRHO validation for Artemis. Status correctly assessed as nominal.

Type partially correct (boxy spacecraft with solar panels) but misses '12U CubeSat' specificity. Components: only solar panels and main body identified; missing antennas, thrusters, star trackers, MLI blanket — major omissions. Appearance: wrong color (gray vs gold/silver), wrong size (~1m vs ~34cm), wrong surface texture (smooth vs wrinkled). Function: vague and incorrect (Earth observation ≠ lunar NRHO validation). Status: correctly assessed as nominal.

Type: Partial credit for identifying it as a small rectangular spacecraft and suggesting CubeSat, but missed the specific 12U class. Components: Only one solar panel mentioned (should be two), and no other key components identified — thrusters, antennas, sensors, or MLI blanket omitted. Appearance: Described as white (incorrect; should be gold/silver MLI) and missing solar panel color/texture details; size estimate is inaccurate (actual ~0.22m x 0.22m x 0.34m). Function: Incorrectly guessed Earth observation; mission is lunar NRHO validation for Artemis/Gateway. Status: Correctly assessed as nominal/intact — full credit.

Type: Partial credit — correctly identifies as small satellite/CubeSat but misses '12U' specification. Components: Only 2/6 key components identified (main bus, solar panels); antennas, thrusters, sensors, and MLI not mentioned or misidentified. Appearance: Incorrect color (white/silver vs gold/silver), wrong size estimate (1m vs ~34cm), and missing solar panel color/grid detail. Function: Vague Earth observation guess — fails to identify lunar NRHO mission or Artemis/Gateway link. Status: Fully correct — no damage, nominal operation accurately assessed.

Type partially correct (CubeSat-class) but misses '12U' specificity and mission name. Components: only solar panels identified; missing MLI, antennas, thrusters, sensors — penalized for omission. Appearance: color wrong (gold/silver vs white/grey), size overestimated (microwave-oven vs 1m), shape correct. Function: vague and incorrect — should specify lunar NRHO/Artemis navigation tech demo. Status: correctly assessed as nominal.

Type partially correct (microsatellite), but misidentified as CubeSat and missed specific platform (MicroStar). Components mostly incorrect: no dish antenna, no white rectangular panel — instead, octagonal bus, solar array on top, two ENA sensors, long antenna, thrusters, shield, radiators. Appearance partially correct (size ~0.5–1m, compact) but shape wrong (octagonal, not boxy), missing key visual features like cylindrical sensors and solar deck. Function completely wrong — IBEX maps heliospheric boundary via ENAs, not Earth observation or comms. Status correctly assessed as nominal.

Type partially correct (small spacecraft) but misclassified as Earth observation; should be microsatellite on MicroStar platform. Key components mostly incorrect: solar panels are surface-mounted on top deck, not large side panels; ENA sensors and thrusters missing; antenna is long, not small. Visual appearance wrong: octagonal, not rectangular; no Earth limb or starfield in ground truth; size mismatch (~bus-tire vs 2m). Function partially correct (science mission) but incorrectly assumes Earth observation instead of heliospheric boundary mapping. Status correctly assessed as nominal.

Type partially correct (small spacecraft) but misses 'microsatellite' and 'MicroStar platform' specificity. Components: only 2/7 identified (solar panel, antenna); thrusters/sensors misidentified as circular ports; ENA imagers, radiators, shield not mentioned. Appearance: shape wrong (rectangular vs octagonal), color inaccurate (dark blue/black vs actual gold/white/silver), size overestimated. Function: completely incorrect — IBEX is for heliospheric boundary mapping, not Earth observation. Status correctly assessed as nominal.

Type partially correct (microsatellite) but incorrectly described as cylindrical instead of octagonal; components misidentified — solar arrays are surface-mounted on top deck, not side panels; ENA sensors are cylindrical but not described accurately; no mention of thrusters or radiators; appearance inaccurately describes shape and color (not dark/golden, no circular features); function is entirely wrong — IBEX maps heliospheric boundary via ENAs, not Earth observation/communications; status correctly assessed as nominal.

Type partially correct (microsatellite) but lacks specific platform name (MicroStar). Components misidentified: only one aperture noted instead of two ENA sensors; solar arrays described as rectangular panels, not surface-mounted on top deck; thrusters and radiators missed entirely. Appearance inaccurately described as box-shaped and dark blue/black — actual is octagonal with gold cylindrical sensors and white/silver surfaces. Function wrongly assumed Earth observation; IBEX maps heliospheric boundary via ENAs. Status correctly assessed as nominal.

Type partially correct (microsatellite), but lacks 'Small Explorer' or 'MicroStar platform' specificity. Components: Only solar panel and antenna are correctly identified; others are fabricated (blue-tinted window, white rectangular sensor) or misidentified — missing ENA sensors, thrusters, radiators. Appearance: Shape is wrong (rectangular vs octagonal), color/texture details inaccurate. Function: Completely incorrect — IBEX maps heliospheric boundary, not Earth observation. Status: Correctly assessed as nominal.

Type is incorrect (misidentified as Earth observation satellite instead of 6U CubeSat). Components partially correct: solar panels and rectangular body noted, but missing critical items like microfluidics payload, LET spectrometer, X-band transponder. Appearance has partial credit for shape and panel deployment, but dimensions and color are inaccurate (actual is ~10x20x30 cm, not 1m x 0.5m x 0.3m; no blue-gray/gold coloring specified in ground truth). Function is wrong — it’s a deep space biology experiment, not Earth observation or comms relay. Status is correctly assessed as nominal.

Type: Partial credit — correctly identifies as small satellite/CubeSat but misses '6U' specification and nano-satellite classification. Components: Only one solar panel noted (should be two wings); omits critical biology payload, LET spectrometer, X-band transponder, and microfluidics cards; boom is not a key component of BioSentinel. Appearance: Correctly notes rectangular shape and compact size, but color description (dark blue/black with gold/yellow) is inaccurate — actual is typically metallic/aluminum with dark solar cells; scale estimate (~0.5-1m) is too large for 6U CubeSat (actual ~30 cm). Function: Incorrect — misattributes mission to Earth observation/tech demo instead of deep space radiation biology experiment with yeast. Status: Mostly correct — nominal status is accurate, no damage observed, but lacks context that it’s operating beyond Earth’s magnetosphere.

Type partially correct (CubeSat mentioned, but not specified as 6U or nano-satellite; loses 5 pts). Components: only one solar panel noted (should be two), no mention of biology payload, LET spectrometer, or X-band transponder (loses 20 pts). Appearance: color and shape partially correct, but size overestimated (~0.5m x 1m vs actual ~0.1m x 0.2m x 0.3m) — loses 5 pts. Function: incorrectly guessed Earth observation/tech demo instead of deep space radiation biology mission — loses 12 pts. Status: correctly assessed as nominal — full credit.

Type: Partial credit for identifying as a CubeSat/microsatellite (correct class), but missed '6U' specification and mission context. Components: Correctly noted central body and solar panels (~5 pts each), but omitted critical biology payload, LET spectrometer, X-band transponder, and onboard electronics. Appearance: Described color and shape vaguely; incorrectly estimated size as ~1m (actual is ~30 cm); missed deployable wing detail. Function: Incorrect — assumed Earth observation/tech demo instead of deep space radiation biology mission. Status: Fully correct — no damage, panels deployed, nominal operation.

Type partially correct (CubeSat derivative) but misclassified as Earth observation; should be 6U CubeSat for deep space biology. Components: only solar panels noted — missing critical biology payload, LET spectrometer, X-band transponder. Appearance: size grossly overestimated (1-2m vs actual 30cm), color inaccurately described (gold cells not typical for BioSentinel). Function: completely wrong — not Earth imaging but deep space radiation biology experiment. Status: correctly assessed as nominal.

Type partially correct (small bus spacecraft) but misses '6U CubeSat' specificity. Components: correctly identifies solar panels and antenna, but omits critical biology payload, LET spectrometer, and misidentifies thermal blanket as a key component. Appearance: shape is correct but color/size estimates are inaccurate (gray/white vs actual metallic/solar panel colors; ~1m length grossly overestimated — actual is ~30 cm). Function: completely wrong — it’s not Earth observation or comms relay but deep space radiation biology. Status: correctly assessed as nominal, but lacks mission-specific context.

Type partially correct (deep space probe) but misclassified as 'large' and conflated with communication satellite — should be 'medium-class, piano-sized, RTG-powered'. Key components: correctly identified dish and boom, but incorrectly described gold blankets (should be aluminum honeycomb), fabricated blue square panel (nonexistent), missed RTG, LORRI, Ralph, Alice, SWAP/PEPSSI. Appearance: wrong shape (not cylindrical/elongated, but triangular/wedge), wrong color (not gold, no thermal blankets mentioned in ground truth), incorrect size estimate. Function: vague but directionally correct; missed specific Pluto/Kuiper Belt mission. Status: fully correct — nominal with no anomalies.

Type partially correct (deep space probe) but overestimates size and misclassifies as 'large' instead of 'medium-class piano-sized'; components include fabricated elements (blue solar panel, gold thermal blanket) — New Horizons has no solar panels and uses RTG; appearance incorrectly describes color (gold vs actual white/dark), shape (boxy vs triangular/wedge), and adds non-existent features; function correctly identifies deep-space science/planetary exploration but lacks specificity about Pluto/Kuiper Belt mission; status correctly assessed as nominal. Partial credit awarded for correct antenna identification and operational status.

Type partially correct (interplanetary probe) but mischaracterized as 'large' instead of 'medium-class piano-sized'; key components include correct dish antenna but falsely claims solar panel and propulsion module, omits RTG and science instruments; visual appearance incorrectly describes gold color and cylindrical body instead of triangular/wedge shape with white dish and dark RTG boom; function is vague but not wrong; status correctly assessed as nominal.

Type: Partial credit for 'interplanetary probe' but incorrect size ('large' vs. 'piano-sized') and missing RTG specification. Components: Correctly identifies dish, boom, main bus — partial credit for 4/7 key components; misses RTG specificity, instruments (LORRI, Ralph, etc.), and solar panel absence. Appearance: Severely inaccurate colors (gold/purple/teal/blue) and shape (cylindrical vs. triangular/wedge); length overestimated. Function: Vague — fails to mention Pluto/Kuiper Belt mission or reconnaissance purpose. Status: Correctly states 'nominal', but ground truth requires no visible damage — diagnosis doesn't justify this claim with visual evidence.

Type partially correct (deep space probe) but mischaracterized as 'large' and 'communication spacecraft' — should be 'medium-class, RTG-powered'. Components: Only the dish antenna is correctly identified; star trackers and thruster are speculative/fabricated; RTG, LORRI, Ralph, Alice, SWAP/PEPSSI missed; solar panels incorrectly implied absent without noting RTG. Appearance: Gold color and boxy shape contradict ground truth (white dish, triangular body); thermal blanket texture not mentioned in truth. Function: Vague telemetry/data relay misses specific Pluto/Kuiper Belt mission. Status: Correctly assessed as nominal, but no mention of ongoing exploration or mission phase.

Type is incorrect (called 'large' instead of 'medium-class piano-sized'); key components partially correct — dish antenna and boom noted, but solar panel falsely claimed (New Horizons has none), thermal blanket color wrong (gold vs actual white/light); appearance misdescribed as rectangular/gold/smooth instead of triangular/wedge-shaped/white/dark RTG; function is vague but not wrong; status correctly assessed as nominal. Partial credit for identifying dish and boom, and correct operational status.

The diagnosis misidentifies the spacecraft type as experimental or conceptual, when it is a confirmed atmospheric entry probe (Huygens). Only partial credit for appearance: correctly notes dome shape and ~2-3m diameter, but misses conical heat shield and gold MLI insulation. No key components are correctly identified — mentions fluted base ring and textured surface which are not in ground truth. Function and status are entirely incorrect; Huygens was fully operational and completed its mission on Titan. Fabricated claims about absence of sensors/antennas/thrusters penalized.

The diagnosis misidentifies the spacecraft type (should be atmospheric entry probe, not educational/test module). Only partial credit for components: mentions dome shape and base skirt, but misses all key scientific instruments, parachutes, and heat shield. Visual appearance partially correct — notes dome shape and size range, but color is wrong (gold/copper MLI, not bright yellow) and omits conical front shield. Function is entirely incorrect — it was not passive or educational, but a Titan descent/landing mission. Status is correctly assessed as nominal, though context is wrong (it landed, not in orbit).

The diagnosis misidentifies the spacecraft type (should be atmospheric entry probe, not satellite), and incorrectly describes function (should be Titan descent/landing mission). Key components are mostly missing — only a 'dome-shaped body' is vaguely correct (back cover shell), but critical elements like heat shield, parachutes, instruments, and MLI insulation are omitted. Visual appearance partially matches (dome shape, color) but underestimates size (2.7m vs 1m) and misses conical front shield. Status is correctly assessed as nominal, though context of mission completion is missing.

Type identification is partially correct (reentry capsule) but misses the specific role as an atmospheric descent probe for Titan. Key components are mostly fabricated or generic (fluted base ring, no solar panels/antennas — which are actually present in Huygens’ design); only partial credit for recognizing absence of propulsion. Visual appearance underestimates size (1.2m vs actual 2.75m), misstates shape (not dome-shaped with fluted base), and omits conical heat shield and gold/copper MLI details. Function is vaguely correct but lacks mission specificity (Titan landing, scientific instruments). Status is correctly assessed as nominal, though mission completion date is omitted.

Type partially correct — identified as atmospheric entry vehicle but missed specific classification as 'atmospheric entry and descent probe' and failed to name Huygens or link to Cassini-Huygens mission. Components mostly incorrect — described generic dome/skirt structure instead of aeroshell, parachutes, instruments, or MLI; no mention of DISR, HASI, GCMS, or SSP. Appearance moderately accurate — correctly noted dome shape and ~2-3m size, but misidentified color as 'bright yellow' (should be gold/copper MLI) and omitted conical heat shield and back cover. Function partially correct — inferred atmospheric entry but missed Titan landing and scientific objectives. Status fully correct — accurately assessed as nominal with no damage.

The diagnosis incorrectly identifies the spacecraft as a 'small experimental satellite' (should be atmospheric entry probe), misstates size (1m vs 2.75m), color (pale yellow vs gold/copper MLI), and function (technology demonstrator vs Titan descent/landing). Only partial credit is awarded for correctly noting a dome-shaped body (back cover) and nominal status (mission complete). No key scientific instruments or parachute system mentioned. Fabricated claims about lack of solar panels/antennas penalized — Huygens had no such features, but this was not diagnostic of its true nature.
